OPENING/WEAK POINT: blast it with the Fire Rod to melt off its ice casing; once melted it drops and flails, and you finish it with the Fire Rod or sword. ITS ATTACK: it fires ice projectiles that form a TRIANGLE on the ground around you — anything caught inside gets frozen and hurt after a beat. Run clear of the triangle before it snaps shut (the Tornado Rod can also lift you away). LOOP: after you damage it, it re-forms its ice shell and the triangle gets bigger and harder to dodge — repeat Fire-Rod-to-melt, then beat it down, until it's destroyed. THE MERGE ANGLE: merging can dodge a ram, but the real answer is Fire Rod + staying out of the triangle.

enemies.name: Zaganaga
enemies.tactic: Desert Palace boss — a spiny cactus-worm that burrows through a huge quicksand pit, surfacing on platforms.
enemies.battle: RENT/EQUIP: the Sand Rod is required (it's the dungeon item — you raise sand pillars to cross the quicksand); a bottled fairy. OPENING: Zaganaga dives into the quicksand and pops up on one of the platforms at random. You can't run on quicksand normally, so use the Sand Rod to raise a path of sand blocks toward whichever platform it surfaced on, get close, and slash its exposed EYE (the weak point). HAZARD: while you cross it spits small flying enemies that try to knock you into the quicksand — one sword hit each clears them. PHASE 2: it moves and spawns enemies faster and adds a sweeping sand-blast aimed at you, but it turns slowly — keep circling with the Sand Rod so the beam can't track you, then close and hit the eye. Don't get greedy or the blast knocks you off. Repeat until it dies. THE MERGE ANGLE: not a wall-merge fight — the Sand Rod traversal is the whole puzzle of reaching it.

enemies.name: Grinexx
enemies.tactic: Turtle Rock boss — a volcano-shelled turtle that swims in lava beneath a grated platform.
enemies.battle: RENT/EQUIP: the Ice Rod is mandatory (it's the dungeon item and its weakness); a bottled fairy. PHASE 1: it swims in the lava under the floor; watch the holes in the grate and hit its head with the Ice Rod as it passes beneath one. Every so often it does a lava-PILLAR attack that bursts up through the holes — stay off the erupting spots, and you can freeze a column with the Ice Rod. PHASE 2: it hauls itself up onto the grate and attacks directly — keep using the Ice Rod on its head (it reacts when hurt) or sword it. TRICK: freeze a lava column so Grinexx lunges its head into the frozen pillar and stuns itself, then unload sword hits. When it spins around the arena, tuck into a corner until it stops. Keep at it until the shell shatters.

enemies.name: Yuga Ganon
enemies.tactic: The finale — Yuga merges with Ganon's power in Lorule Castle, fighting first with a trident, then absorbing the Triforce of Wisdom for a Dead Man's Volley.
enemies.battle: RENT/EQUIP: ideally OWN (don't just rent) your sword-reliant kit and bring 1-2 bottled fairies and lots of hearts/potions — dying here is brutal and you don't want Sheerow stripping rented gear mid-finale. PHASE 1 (Trident): Yuga teleports around the round room and stabs/slashes with his trident; the safe spot is BEHIND him — when he attacks, dodge to his back and slash. As he weakens he double-slashes, hurls and recatches the trident (big damage on contact — avoid its path), and rains energy from the ceiling (just keep moving). PHASE 2 (Dead Man's Volley): he absorbs the Triforce of Wisdom and lobs a big energy ball — swing your sword to bat it back, and rally it until he fails to return it. THE MERGE ANGLE — the finish: Zelda hands you the BOW OF LIGHT. Now merge into the wall, face Yuga (who's also in the wall), and fire a Light Arrow; he tries to block it, which exposes his back, so emerge, get behind him, and strike to force him out of the wall and open to your sword. Repeat the merge-and-Light-Arrow sequence to win.

basics.title: Sword & charged Spin Attack
basics.body: Your sword is your bread and butter — tap the button to swing, and hold it to charge a Spin Attack that hits everything around you (great for crowds or for breaking a foe's guard). Z-target (lock on) with L to keep an enemy centred so your swings and dodges always face the right way. Don't just mash; many foes guard, so wait for an opening — after they swing or while they're stunned — then strike.

basics.title: The Shield
basics.body: Hold the shield button to block frontal attacks — most basic enemy swings, arrows, and projectiles bounce right off as long as you're facing them. It does nothing against attacks from behind or from above, so keep your front to the threat. Against shielded enemies, a Spin Attack or a bomb can break their guard so your sword can land.

basics.title: The green ENERGY meter (pace it!)
basics.body: Almost every item you rent from Ravio — the Bow, Bombs, Fire/Ice/Tornado/Sand Rod, Hookshot, Hammer — and even wall-merging draw from one shared green energy meter, NOT from limited ammo. You'll never 'run out of arrows', but spam an item and the meter empties and locks that item out until it refills — which it does automatically on its own after a moment (purple pots also drop Energy Potions to top it off). So pace yourself: fire, let it recharge a beat, fire again — don't dump the whole bar at once mid-fight.

basics.title: Wall-merge: travel AND dodge
basics.body: Ravio's Bracelet lets you press into a wall and slide along it as a flat painting. It's not just for puzzles and shortcuts — it's a defensive tool. Merge into a wall and most attacks pass harmlessly over the spot where you were standing, so you can use it to escape a charge, a beam, or a boss's slam. Several bosses are built around this: merge to bait a punch into the wall, or merge onto a boss's shield to slip behind it. The merge also sips the energy meter, so don't hold it forever.

basics.title: Rented items get repossessed if you DIE
basics.body: Ravio's shop lets you RENT items cheaply up front, and you keep using a rented item as long as you like — until you get a Game Over, at which point Ravio's bird Sheerow swoops in and takes back every rented item (you can rent it again, but it's a setback). Once you've collected all three Pendants — or pulled the Master Sword — Ravio lets you BUY items to own them permanently, and bought items survive death. Tip: buy the items you rely on most (Bow, Bombs, Hammer, the Rod a dungeon needs) so a bad fight can't strip your whole kit. The very FIRST item you buy is half-price (a one-time discount), so spend it on something pricey like a Rod.

basics.title: Bottled fairy = auto-revive
basics.body: If you catch a fairy in an empty bottle, it sits there until you need it. Take a hit that would drop your hearts to zero and the fairy is automatically released, reviving you with several hearts restored — no menu, no input needed. It's the single best insurance against losing your rented gear, so always head into a tough dungeon or boss with at least one bottled fairy. Bottles also hold potions; a fairy is the one that saves you from a Game Over.

COMPENDIUM.name: Forgotten Sword
COMPENDIUM.effect: Link's first and weakest blade — a simple one-handed sword for basic slashes and the spin attack. It carries you until you claim the Master Sword, after which you never need it again.
COMPENDIUM.where: Also called the Captain's Sword. The Captain forgets it at the Blacksmith's Forge, and the Blacksmith's wife asks Link to return it. Link follows the Captain to the Sanctuary, but Seres screams and the doors lock; Dampe reveals a secret passage under a grave in the graveyard. Link sneaks in to find Yuga has turned the Captain into a painting — so the sword stays Link's to keep. A found story item, not a Ravio rental.

COMPENDIUM.name: Master Sword
COMPENDIUM.effect: The legendary sword of evil's bane — far stronger than the Forgotten Sword and the blade you'll use for most of the game. When Link is at full health it fires a sword beam at range.
COMPENDIUM.where: Pulled from its pedestal in the Lost Woods after collecting the three Pendants (Power, Wisdom, Courage). A found story item, not rented. Can be upgraded twice with Master Ore.

COMPENDIUM.name: Master Sword Lv2
COMPENDIUM.effect: The first Master Sword upgrade — its attack power is doubled, cutting through tougher Lorule enemies much faster. Still throws a sword beam at full health.
COMPENDIUM.where: Take 2 pieces of Master Ore to the Blacksmith's Forge in Hyrule and he reforges the Master Sword into Lv2. The four Master Ore are hidden in Lorule — in the Dark Palace, Skull Woods, and Thieves' Hideout dungeons, plus one under the Lorule Graveyard.

COMPENDIUM.name: Master Sword Lv3
COMPENDIUM.effect: The Master Sword at maximum strength — attack power is doubled again over Lv2 (four times the base sword), the strongest weapon in the game. This is ALBW's equal of A Link to the Past's Golden/Tempered Sword.
COMPENDIUM.where: Bring the last 2 pieces of Master Ore to the Blacksmith in Lorule, who forges Lv3. Requires all 4 Master Ore total (2 for Lv2 in Hyrule, 2 for Lv3 in Lorule).

COMPENDIUM.name: Shield
COMPENDIUM.effect: A basic shield held in front of Link (press R) while not attacking — blocks ordinary enemy attacks and non-magic projectiles from the front. Does not protect against fire or magic.
COMPENDIUM.where: Bought for 50 Rupees from any Item Shop (the standard village shops, e.g. in Kakariko) — NOT one of Ravio's rentals. Since it's a purchase, it's yours permanently and is never lost on death.

COMPENDIUM.name: Hylian Shield
COMPENDIUM.effect: The best shield in the game. On top of normal blocking it deflects fire- and magic-based projectiles (Beamos beams, Lynel fire breath), and it can never be destroyed (even a Like Like can't eat it).
COMPENDIUM.where: A found dungeon item — in a chest inside Turtle Rock (Lorule), reached after the Big Key by merging into a wall to push over a grate bridge in the central room. Optional, not required to finish the dungeon; not a Ravio rental.

COMPENDIUM.name: Blue Mail
COMPENDIUM.effect: An armor upgrade over the starting green tunic — halves the damage Link takes from every hit. Worn automatically once obtained.
COMPENDIUM.where: A found dungeon item. Locations are order-dependent: whichever of the two armor chests you open first (the big chest in the Swamp Palace, or the one in Lorule Castle) gives the Blue Mail; the other then holds the Red Mail. Not rented or upgraded by Maiamai.

COMPENDIUM.name: Red Mail
COMPENDIUM.effect: The best armor in the game — cuts incoming damage to a quarter, twice as protective as the Blue Mail and the final defensive upgrade.
COMPENDIUM.where: A found dungeon item, in the other of the two armor chests (Lorule Castle's 4th-floor big chest, or the Swamp Palace big chest) — whichever you DIDN'T already open for the Blue Mail. The Lorule Castle chest hides behind an invisible platform revealed by extinguishing the nearby torches. Not rented or upgraded by Maiamai.

COMPENDIUM.name: Bow
COMPENDIUM.effect: Fires arrows to hit distant enemies, switches, and eyes. Like all of Ravio's gear it draws from the shared green energy meter instead of consuming arrow ammo, so you can keep firing as the meter refills.
COMPENDIUM.where: Rented for 50 Rupees from Ravio's Shop (set up in Link's house), then buyable outright for 800 Rupees after the first three Pendants. Returns to Ravio via Sheerow if Link falls while it's only rented. Buy it to keep it permanently and unlock the Nice Bow upgrade.

COMPENDIUM.name: Nice Bow
COMPENDIUM.effect: The Maiamai-upgraded Bow — fires three arrows at once in a small spread, making it easy to hit a target (or several enemies) without precise aim and hitting harder overall. Still runs on the energy meter.
COMPENDIUM.where: Upgraded by Mother Maiamai (in the cave behind her grotto) after you have bought the Bow outright from Ravio and returned 10 of her lost Maiamai children. A found upgrade, not rented.

COMPENDIUM.name: Bow of Light
COMPENDIUM.effect: A sacred bow that fires Light Arrows — the only weapon that can harm Yuga Ganon. While he's merged into a wall, merge into the wall yourself and shoot a Light Arrow at his back to force him out, then strike with the sword; repeat across the fight to win.
COMPENDIUM.where: Given to Link by Princess Zelda (through her painting) during the final Yuga Ganon battle, after the descendants of the Seven Sages and the Triforce of Courage are gathered. A one-time story gift, not from Ravio or Maiamai.

COMPENDIUM.name: Ravio's Bracelet
COMPENDIUM.effect: Lets Link merge into walls as a living painting, sliding flat along any surface to cross gaps, slip behind bars, and reach areas no other route allows. It is the game's signature mechanic and is needed almost everywhere.
COMPENDIUM.where: Given for free by Ravio early on after he sets up his rental shop inside Link's House; a later story event upgrades it so the merge can also be used in Lorule. Not a rental and not upgraded by Mother Maiamai.

COMPENDIUM.name: Bombs
COMPENDIUM.effect: Sets down a bomb that explodes after a moment to blast cracked walls, stun enemies, and hit switches. Draws from the shared green energy meter instead of a finite ammo count, so it recharges automatically.
COMPENDIUM.where: Rented or bought from Ravio's Shop. Buy it outright and Mother Maiamai can upgrade it into Nice Bombs, which have a larger blast radius.

COMPENDIUM.name: Fire Rod
COMPENDIUM.effect: Shoots a burst of flame that burns enemies, lights torches, and melts ice. Costs energy from the shared meter per use.
COMPENDIUM.where: Rented or bought from Ravio's Shop. Owned (bought) copies can be upgraded by Mother Maiamai into the Nice Fire Rod, which unleashes a much larger pillar of fire.

COMPENDIUM.name: Ice Rod
COMPENDIUM.effect: Fires ice that freezes enemies solid (letting you use them as stepping platforms) and can freeze certain surfaces. Uses the shared energy meter.
COMPENDIUM.where: Rented or bought from Ravio's Shop. Bring Mother Maiamai 10 Maiamai while you own it and she upgrades it into the Nice Ice Rod, which freezes a wider area.

COMPENDIUM.name: Tornado Rod
COMPENDIUM.effect: Whips up a small whirlwind that flips over shielded or armored enemies and launches Link upward to reach higher ledges. Drains the shared energy meter.
COMPENDIUM.where: Rented or bought from Ravio's Shop (it is NOT a House of Gales dungeon prize — you rent it BEFORE that dungeon, since it's required to enter and clear the House of Gales). If owned, Mother Maiamai upgrades it into the Nice Tornado Rod with a bigger gust.

COMPENDIUM.name: Sand Rod
COMPENDIUM.effect: Raises pillars of sand out of sand-covered floors, creating platforms and walls and exposing hidden paths. Uses the shared energy meter.
COMPENDIUM.where: Rented from Ravio's Shop after clearing the Thieves' Hideout and rescuing Osfala (it is NOT the Desert Palace dungeon item — that dungeon's prize is the Titan's Mitt; the Sand Rod is needed to navigate the Desert Palace). Mother Maiamai can upgrade it into the Nice Sand Rod, which raises wider, longer pillars.

COMPENDIUM.name: Hammer
COMPENDIUM.effect: Pounds in stakes, flattens enemies, and smashes obstacles. Uses energy from the shared meter on each swing.
COMPENDIUM.where: Rented or bought from Ravio's Shop. Buy it and Mother Maiamai can make it the Nice Hammer, with a stronger, wider strike.

COMPENDIUM.name: Hookshot
COMPENDIUM.effect: Fires a chain that grabs onto posts, chests, and certain enemies, pulling Link across gaps or yanking objects toward him. Costs energy per shot.
COMPENDIUM.where: Rented or bought from Ravio's Shop. Owned copies are upgraded by Mother Maiamai into the Nice Hookshot, which has a longer reach.

COMPENDIUM.name: Boomerang
COMPENDIUM.effect: Thrown to stun enemies, grab distant items, and hit switches before returning to Link. Uses the shared energy meter.
COMPENDIUM.where: Rented or bought from Ravio's Shop. Buy it and Mother Maiamai can upgrade it to the Nice Boomerang, which flies a wider arc and pulls in items over a larger area.

COMPENDIUM.name: Lamp
COMPENDIUM.effect: Lights torches to open doors and burns away simple obstacles, and provides light in dark rooms. Uses the shared energy meter.
COMPENDIUM.where: Rented or bought from Ravio's Shop. The Lamp does not have a Mother Maiamai 'Nice' upgrade.

COMPENDIUM.name: Net
COMPENDIUM.effect: Catches small flying creatures like bees and fairies and can swat certain projectiles back at enemies.
COMPENDIUM.where: Rented or bought from Ravio's Shop. The Net does not have a 'Nice' upgrade.

COMPENDIUM.name: Pegasus Boots
COMPENDIUM.effect: Hold the run button to dash at high speed, charging through enemies and breakable objects and crossing long stretches quickly.
COMPENDIUM.where: Won from the Shady Guy (the thief) in Kakariko Village: merge into the wall behind him, sneak up, and pop out to scare him into handing them over. Not a rental and has no 'Nice' upgrade. (With the sword equipped while dashing, this enables the Dash Attack.)

COMPENDIUM.name: Power Glove
COMPENDIUM.effect: Lets Link pick up and throw small rocks and bushes that block paths or hide secrets.
COMPENDIUM.where: Given by Rosso at his house (the Miner's House) after you clear the Eastern Palace. A permanent item; later replaced by the stronger Titan's Mitt (no 'Nice' upgrade).

COMPENDIUM.name: Titan's Mitt
COMPENDIUM.effect: An upgraded Power Glove that lets Link lift the largest boulders and dark rocks, opening late-game paths the Power Glove cannot.
COMPENDIUM.where: The dungeon item of the Desert Palace (in Lorule). Permanently upgrades the Power Glove; no 'Nice' version.

COMPENDIUM.name: Flippers
COMPENDIUM.effect: Allow Link to swim across deep water instead of drowning, opening up lakes, rivers, and water routes in both worlds.
COMPENDIUM.where: Given by Queen Oren of the Zora after you recover her stolen Smooth Gem (buy it back from the Kakariko street merchant and return it to her in Zora's Domain). Permanent; no 'Nice' upgrade.

COMPENDIUM.name: Bell
COMPENDIUM.effect: Ring it to summon Irene on her broom and instantly warp to any Weather Vane you have already activated, making travel across Hyrule and Lorule fast and easy.
COMPENDIUM.where: Given by Irene (the witch's granddaughter) after you clear the Eastern Palace. Not a rental; no 'Nice' upgrade. Weather Vanes must be touched once to unlock them as warp destinations.

COMPENDIUM.name: Pendants of Virtue
COMPENDIUM.effect: The three sacred pendants — the Pendant of Courage, the Pendant of Power, and the Pendant of Wisdom. Collecting all three lets Link claim the Master Sword.
COMPENDIUM.where: Each is the reward for clearing one of the three Hyrule dungeons: the Eastern Palace, the House of Gales, and the Tower of Hera. Story key items, not rented or upgraded.

COMPENDIUM.name: Seven Sages
COMPENDIUM.effect: Seven descendants of the original Sages, sealed inside paintings by Yuga across Lorule. Rescuing all seven opens the path to the final confrontation with Yuga-Ganon.
COMPENDIUM.where: Each Sage is freed by clearing one of the seven Lorule dungeons. They are story objectives tracked in the menu, not items you rent or buy.

COMPENDIUM.name: Bottle
COMPENDIUM.effect: An empty bottle that stores one consumable — a potion, fairy, bee, or other item — for use when needed. Multiple bottles let Link carry several lifesaving items at once.
COMPENDIUM.where: There are FIVE bottles: from the Kakariko Village street merchant (100 Rupees); from the Bird Lover under the bridge near Lake Hylia (after the Flippers); from the Stranded Man on Death Mountain (deliver him premium milk from Kakariko's milk bar); from a chest in the Vacant House in Lorule (bomb the back wall); and from the Great Rupee Fairy in Lorule's Thieves' Town (donate 3000 Rupees total). Permanent; not rented or upgraded.

COMPENDIUM.name: Lost Maiamai
COMPENDIUM.effect: One hundred tiny lost Maiamai children hidden throughout Hyrule and Lorule. Returning them to Mother Maiamai (10 at a time) lets her upgrade your owned items into stronger 'Nice' versions; collecting all 100 unlocks the Great Spin Attack.
COMPENDIUM.where: Scattered across both worlds, often revealed by merging into walls, lifting rocks, or using items in the right spot. Carry them back to Mother Maiamai's cave beneath Lake Hylia.

COMPENDIUM.name: Piece of Heart
COMPENDIUM.effect: A quarter of a heart container. Collect four Pieces of Heart and Link's maximum health permanently increases by one full heart.
COMPENDIUM.where: Hidden throughout Hyrule and Lorule as exploration rewards, minigame prizes, and quest rewards (28 in all). Permanent collectibles, not rented.

COMPENDIUM.name: Heart Container
COMPENDIUM.effect: Instantly adds one full heart to Link's maximum health.
COMPENDIUM.where: Awarded for defeating each dungeon's boss. A guaranteed story reward, one per major boss.

COMPENDIUM.name: Master Ore
COMPENDIUM.effect: Rare ore used to temper the Master Sword. Two pieces let the Hyrule Blacksmith upgrade it to the stronger Master Sword Lv2 (red); two more let the Lorule Blacksmith forge the Master Sword Lv3 (golden), the strongest sword in the game.
COMPENDIUM.where: Four pieces are hidden in Lorule, in big chests inside the Thieves' Hideout, Skull Woods, the Dark Palace, and the Sanctuary of Lorule (reached via the Fissure inside Hyrule's Sanctuary). Take them to the Blacksmith to upgrade.

COMPENDIUM.name: Monster Tail
COMPENDIUM.effect: A monster drop used as a potion ingredient. Bring 10 to the Witch and she brews a Blue Potion, which refills all of Link's hearts (the best healing potion in the game).
COMPENDIUM.where: Dropped by enemies and found in grass, pots, and skulls throughout Hyrule and Lorule. Deliver to the Witch at her hut (she also charges a Rupee fee to brew).

COMPENDIUM.name: Monster Horn
COMPENDIUM.effect: A monster drop used as a potion ingredient. Bring 10 to the Witch and she brews a Yellow Potion, which makes Link temporarily invincible to all enemy attacks.
COMPENDIUM.where: Dropped by enemies and found in grass, pots, and skulls across both worlds. Deliver to the Witch at her hut.

COMPENDIUM.name: Monster Guts
COMPENDIUM.effect: A monster drop used as a potion ingredient. Bring 10 to the Witch and she brews a Purple Potion, which damages every nearby enemy when drunk.
COMPENDIUM.where: Commonly dropped by enemies and found in grass, pots, and skulls throughout Hyrule and Lorule. Deliver to the Witch at her hut.

COMPENDIUM.name: Rupees
COMPENDIUM.effect: The game's money, used to buy and rent items from Ravio, pay the Witch and Blacksmith, and play minigames. Comes in colored values from the green 1-Rupee up to large silver and gold Rupees.
COMPENDIUM.where: Found in grass, pots, and chests, dropped by enemies, and won at minigames throughout both worlds. Not rented or upgraded.

quests.name: Free Mother Maiamai (start the hunt)
quests.location: A cave on the northwest shore of Lake Hylia, Hyrule
quests.reward: Unlocks the 100-Maiamai hunt and the Nice-item upgrades
quests.oneLine: Open the giant Maiamai's hidden cave to begin collecting her 100 lost children.
quests.how: You can't get in until you have Bombs (rent or buy them from Ravio). Go to the northwest shore of Lake Hylia and bomb the cracked rock to open the cave, then talk to Mother Maiamai inside. From now on she upgrades your items for every 10 baby Maiamai you bring back, so this is the gate for the whole hunt.

quests.name: Collect all 100 Lost Maiamai
quests.location: Scattered across the overworlds of both Hyrule and Lorule (under rocks, in water, behind merge-walls, in grass and trees)
quests.reward: An item upgrade every 10 found; the Great Spin Attack at 100
quests.oneLine: Hunt down the 100 hidden baby Maiamai squeaking across both worlds.
quests.how: Each Maiamai cries out and gets louder as you get close, so keep the game sound on and listen for the squeak, then lift a rock, bomb a wall, burn a tree, merge into a wall, or swim to reach it. Many need a specific item, so come back as you unlock more gear. There are exactly 50 in Hyrule and 50 in Lorule; a location map saves a lot of pixel-hunting if you stall.

quests.name: Nice-item upgrades (every 10 Maiamai)
quests.location: Mother Maiamai's cave, northwest Lake Hylia
quests.reward: A 'Nice' version of one owned item per 10 Maiamai (9 upgrades by 90)
quests.oneLine: Trade Maiamai in tens to super-charge the items you actually OWN.
quests.how: Every 10 Maiamai lets Mother Maiamai upgrade one item into its 'Nice' form, but ONLY for items you've BOUGHT from Ravio, not ones you're merely renting, so buy first if you want the upgrade. There are nine upgradeable items: Bow, Bombs, Hookshot, Boomerang, Hammer, Fire Rod, Ice Rod, Tornado Rod, and Sand Rod. The Nice Bow fires three arrows at once, Nice Bombs blast wider, and the rest hit bigger and harder. You need 90 Maiamai to upgrade all nine.

quests.name: The Great Spin Attack (100 Maiamai)
quests.location: Mother Maiamai's cave, northwest Lake Hylia
quests.reward: Great Spin Attack (a wider, stronger spin that briefly makes Link invincible)
quests.oneLine: Return the final Maiamai for the ultimate sword flourish.
quests.how: Bring back all 100 babies and Mother Maiamai upgrades your Spin Attack to the Great Spin, which roughly doubles the radius and keeps you invincible for most of the swing. It's a pure bonus combat upgrade, the cherry on top of finishing the hunt rather than something the main quest needs.

quests.name: Octoball Derby (baseball)
quests.location: Lorule, south of the Blacksmith and southeast of Thieves' Town
quests.reward: Rupees per hit; a Piece of Heart for scoring 100+ rupees
quests.oneLine: Zelda's take on baseball — bat 30 pitches into the pots for a heart.
quests.how: Pay to play, then swing your sword to bat each of the 30 pitches, aiming to smash the clay pots dotted around the field (each pot is worth rupees). Score 100 rupees or more and the Derby Boy gives you a Piece of Heart; below that you just keep the rupees you knocked loose. Timing your swing to meet the ball squarely is the whole game — a centered hit carries far enough to clear distant pots.

quests.name: Cucco Ranch (dodge the cuccos)
quests.location: Hyrule, just south of Kakariko Village
quests.reward: 150 rupees and a Piece of Heart (on the Rooster level)
quests.oneLine: Survive a swarm of cuccos for 30 seconds to win a heart.
quests.how: You're dropped in a pen and must avoid getting pecked for 30 seconds — just run smooth circles around the edge so the cuccos chase your tail and never catch up. Work up through Egg and Chick to the harder Rooster level; clearing Rooster pays 150 rupees AND a Piece of Heart together. After that, repeat clears just pay out rupees.

quests.name: Rupee Rush — Hyrule
quests.location: Hyrule, south of Kakariko Village
quests.reward: A Piece of Heart for 100+ points
quests.oneLine: Grab as many rupees as you can in 30 unmarked seconds.
quests.how: Pay 50 rupees, then sweep the yard before the hidden 30-second timer ends — there's no on-screen clock, so count in your head, and if you finish after time's up you forfeit everything you grabbed. You don't dig: use the Tornado Rod to flip the floor tiles, the Hammer on the boulder, and mow the grass near the entrance, then collect what pops out. Score 100 points or more to win the Piece of Heart.

quests.name: Rupee Rush — Lorule
quests.location: Lorule, south of Thieves' Town (the dark-world counterpart of the Hyrule spot)
quests.reward: A second Piece of Heart for 150+ points
quests.oneLine: The dark-world Rupee Rush — pricier, higher target, a second heart.
quests.how: Same Tornado-Rod-and-Hammer game in Lorule, but the fee is 100 rupees and the winning line is 150 points instead of 100. Same rules: count the unspoken 30 seconds and finish on time. Clearing it nets a separate Piece of Heart from the Hyrule version, so both are worth doing.

quests.name: Hyrule Hotfoot (the foot race)
quests.location: Hyrule — the course starts northeast of Lake Hylia and ends at the Miner's House
quests.reward: 100 rupees on the first clear; a Piece of Heart on the faster time
quests.oneLine: Sprint the marked course against the clock for a heart.
quests.how: Race the marked overworld course on foot. Beat the gentler 75-second target for 100 rupees, then come back and beat the tighter 65-second time to claim the Piece of Heart (an Advanced 'beat your best time' mode then pays 300 rupees per win). Pegasus Boots make this far easier — dash the straights and ease off only for turns. Note: you'll be disqualified if you warp with the Bell to shortcut the route.

quests.name: Treacherous Tower — Intermediate (Heart Piece)
quests.location: Lorule, on Death Mountain (the Lorule counterpart of the Tower of Hera)
quests.reward: A Piece of Heart (first Intermediate clear)
quests.oneLine: Clear the 15-floor combat tier once for a heart.
quests.how: Pay 200 rupees and fight through all 15 floors of the Intermediate gauntlet, clearing every enemy on a floor to open the stairs up. Your first clear earns a Piece of Heart (plus a 1,000-rupee audience payout). Rent strong items — the Bow and Bombs trivialize many floors — and keep a Bottle with a fairy for insurance. See the Treacherous Tower entries under Combat Challenges for the full tier breakdown.

quests.name: Catch the Shady Guy → Pegasus Boots
quests.location: Kakariko Village, Hyrule
quests.reward: Pegasus Boots (hold to dash)
quests.oneLine: Corner the thief who stole the Zora Queen's gem to claim the dash boots.
quests.how: While dealing with Queen Oren in Zora's Domain you learn her Smooth Gem was stolen by a Shady Guy; track him to Kakariko Village, but he bolts the moment he sees your face. Merge into the wall behind him (he can't spot a painting) and slide around to grab him from behind. Caught, he gives you the Pegasus Boots out of guilt — hold a button to break into a charging dash. (He'd already sold the gem, so to finish Oren's errand you buy it back from the Street Merchant for 200 rupees and return it for the Zora's Flippers.)

quests.name: The Bee Guy & the Net
quests.location: Kakariko Village, Hyrule — the house marked with a bee sign
quests.reward: The Net (bug-catching), plus rupee payouts for bottled bees
quests.oneLine: The bee-loving man hands you a Net and pays for the bees you bottle.
quests.how: Enter the bee-sign house in Kakariko with at least one Empty Bottle and the Bee Guy gives you the Net for catching bugs. Bring him bottled bees for rupees — a normal Bee is a small payout, a rare GOLDEN Bee is worth a lot. Give him a Golden Bee and he hands over the Bee Badge, which makes wild bees you disturb fight FOR you instead of stinging you. A bottled Golden Bee can escape if you're careless, so deliver it promptly.

quests.name: The Bottles hunt
quests.location: Across Hyrule and Lorule (a merchant, the Bird Lover, a Death Mountain trade, a Lorule chest, and the Great Rupee Fairy)
quests.reward: All 5 Empty Bottles (fairies, potions, bees)
quests.oneLine: Track down all five Empty Bottles to carry fairies and potions.
quests.how: There are five Bottles: buy one from the Street Merchant in Kakariko (100 rupees); talk to the Bird Lover east of Link's House and pick the 'came to chat' option; give Premium Milk to the man stranded on Death Mountain; bomb the back wall of the Vacant House in Lorule for a chest; and donate 3,000 rupees to the Great Rupee Fairy in Lorule. Each Bottle stores a fairy (auto-revive), a potion, or a bee, so all five meaningfully boost survivability.

quests.name: The Golden Bee shop → Bee Badge
quests.location: Lorule — a cave in the Skull Woods area
quests.reward: A Golden Bee → the Bee Badge (passive: bees protect you)
quests.oneLine: Buy a guaranteed Golden Bee, then trade it for the Bee Badge.
quests.how: If you can't catch a Golden Bee in the wild, the Mysterious Man in a cave in Lorule's Skull Woods sells one for 888 rupees. Bottle it and give it to the Bee Guy in Kakariko to receive the Bee Badge. With the Badge equipped, wild bees you disturb sting your enemies instead of you — handy, but firmly an optional luxury.

quests.name: Find the 4 Master Ore
quests.location: Hyrule & Lorule — Thieves' Hideout, Dark Palace, Skull Woods, and the Lorule Sanctuary (reached via a fissure in the Hyrule Sanctuary)
quests.reward: Master Ore (4 total) to upgrade the Master Sword twice
quests.oneLine: Gather the four chunks of Master Ore that power up the legendary blade.
quests.how: Master Ore exists only to upgrade the Master Sword, and there are exactly four pieces: in the Thieves' Hideout, Dark Palace, and Skull Woods dungeons, plus one reached through the fissure inside the Sanctuary (which drops you into the Lorule Sanctuary). Each is a fixed chest find, not a random drop. You don't need the sword maxed to beat the game, but it makes the late fights much faster, so it's a strong optional goal.

quests.name: Master Sword Lv2 (Tempered)
quests.location: The Blacksmith's house, eastern Hyrule
quests.reward: Master Sword Lv2 (double the attack power)
quests.oneLine: Hand the Hyrule smith 2 Master Ore to temper the Master Sword.
quests.how: Once you have the Master Sword and 2 Master Ore, take them to the Blacksmith in Hyrule and he forges the Master Sword Lv2 — this doubles the blade's attack power. It uses two of your four ore, so it's the first of the two upgrades. Do it before the late dungeons and the extra power smooths out a lot of fights.

quests.name: Master Sword Lv3
quests.location: The Blacksmith's counterpart in Lorule
quests.reward: Master Sword Lv3 (the strongest blade)
quests.oneLine: Bring the last 2 Master Ore to the Lorule smith for the strongest sword.
quests.how: After Lv2, take 2 more Master Ore to the Blacksmith on the Lorule side and he forges the Master Sword Lv3 — doubling the power again for the strongest sword in the game (and a stronger full-health sword beam). This spends your final two ore, so all four are used across the two upgrades. With Lv3 in hand, the endgame bosses fall noticeably faster.

quests.name: Treacherous Tower — Beginner
quests.location: Lorule, Death Mountain (the Lorule counterpart of the Tower of Hera)
quests.reward: 300 rupees (unlocks Intermediate)
quests.oneLine: The 5-floor warm-up tier of the combat gauntlet.
quests.how: Pay 100 rupees and clear all five floors — defeat every enemy on a floor to open the stairs up. Winning pays 300 rupees (a tidy profit) and unlocks the Intermediate tier. It's an easy introduction; bring any rented weapon and you'll be fine.

quests.name: Treacherous Tower — Intermediate
quests.location: Lorule, Death Mountain
quests.reward: A Piece of Heart (first clear) plus 1,000 rupees; rupees on repeats
quests.oneLine: The 15-floor middle tier — clear once for a heart.
quests.how: Costs 200 rupees and runs 15 floors of escalating enemy rooms. The first clear awards a Piece of Heart and a 1,000-rupee payout (net 800 profit), and beating it unlocks the Advanced tier. Rent the Bow and Bombs for crowd floors and keep a fairy in a Bottle as insurance against a bad room.

quests.name: Treacherous Tower — Advanced
quests.location: Lorule, Death Mountain
quests.reward: Super Lamp (1st clear), then Super Net (2nd clear); ~5,000 rupees each clear
quests.oneLine: The brutal 50-floor tier — best rupee farm and two unique upgrades.
quests.how: The marathon tier: 300 rupees to enter, FIFTY floors. Your first full clear yields the Super Lamp (a far stronger fire beam) and a ~5,000-rupee haul (net ~4,700), and a SECOND clear gives the Super Net (a stronger Net). Go in with an upgraded sword, Nice items, plenty of healing, and bottled fairies — it's as much an endurance test as a skill test, so it's a natural late-game goal.

quests.name: Set up your Shadow Link (StreetPass)
quests.location: The empty field on the west side of Kakariko Village
quests.reward: Enables Shadow Link battles
quests.oneLine: Register your own Shadow Link with Gramps to turn on StreetPass duels.
quests.how: Talk to Gramps in the field on the west side of Kakariko and choose Setup — you pick the two items he'll carry (just one if you haven't expanded your pouch yet) and your heart count and gear become the bounty other players fight. After setup, StreetPass keeps working with the game closed, and any Shadow Links you receive appear as signs in that field. This only registers your fighter; the actual battles are the next entry.

quests.name: Beat Shadow Link battles (and the offline samples)
quests.location: The field on the west side of Kakariko Village
quests.reward: Rupees by bounty (50–999); medals for the 50 challenges
quests.oneLine: Duel AI Shadow Links for rupees and challenge medals — even with no friends nearby.
quests.how: Walk into a Shadow Link sign to fight an AI Link in one of five arenas; you can only use the two items it carries, so plan your loadout. Winning pays a bounty (50–999 rupees by difficulty) and earns medals toward the 50 challenges — collect all 50 and you can finally duel Gramps himself, who wields upgraded gear. NO STREETPASS PARTNER? The game seeds FREE sample Shadow Links (named 'Shade') into your file periodically as you play, so you can chase every reward entirely offline.

GREAT_FAIRIES.name: Mother Maiamai
GREAT_FAIRIES.location: In a cave at Lake Hylia, just southeast of Link's house. The entrance is sealed by a boulder — blow it open with Bombs (rent or buy from Ravio). Inside, Mother Maiamai has lost her 100 baby Maiamai, hidden all over both Hyrule AND Lorule (behind cracked walls, under rocks, in water, on the map's edges). Many need a specific item — Hammer, Bombs, Sand Rod, Ice Rod, wall-merge, etc. — to reach. You don't have to return them in fixed batches; she gives a reward for every 10 you hand in.
GREAT_FAIRIES.cost: FREE — she rewards you for bringing back her children. Every 10 Lost Maiamai you return = one item upgraded to its stronger 'Nice' version. It must be an item you BOUGHT from Ravio (only possible after the 3 Pendants), not one you're merely renting. The 9 upgradeable Ravio items: Bow, Bombs, Fire Rod, Ice Rod, Tornado Rod, Hammer, Sand Rod, Boomerang, Hookshot — so 90 Maiamai cover all nine. Nice versions are bigger/stronger: the Nice Bow fires 3 arrows at once; Nice Bombs are about twice as big with twice the power; the Nice Fire Rod's flame pillar is taller, wider and reaches farther; the Nice Ice Rod drops 4 icicles instead of 1; Nice Sand Rod pillars last longer (and puff a line of dust on hard ground); Nice Boomerang/Hookshot travel faster and farther (the Nice Hookshot also deals damage). They still draw from the same recharging Energy Gauge. Return all 100 and she grants the Great Spin — a much larger, stronger spin attack.

GREAT_FAIRIES.name: Great Rupee Fairy
GREAT_FAIRIES.location: In a hidden cave in Lorule, northwest of the Bomb Flower Store (the dark-world counterpart of the area where you get the Big Bomb Flower). A large cracked boulder blocks the fountain — clear it with the Big Bomb Flower to open the way. (Lorule is reached by wall-merging through fissures in Hyrule using Ravio's Bracelet.)
GREAT_FAIRIES.cost: Toss in Rupees — 50 or 200 at a time. Donations heal your hearts and release a few free fairies along the way. Donate 3,000 Rupees in total and she grows to full size and gives you a Bottle (one of the game's five). After she hands over the Bottle you can no longer donate or see her, so it's a one-time payoff — worth it only if you have Rupees to spare.

GREAT_FAIRIES.name: Hyrule Blacksmith (Master Sword Lv2)
GREAT_FAIRIES.location: The Blacksmith's house in eastern Hyrule, near Hyrule Castle (east of Link's house). Bring him 2 Master Ore. There are 4 Master Ore in the whole game, found in big chests in the Lorule dungeons/areas: Dark Palace, Thieves' Hideout, Skull Woods, and the Lorule Sanctuary. Several (not all) need wall-merge to reach.
GREAT_FAIRIES.cost: FREE — no Rupee cost. Give the Hyrule Blacksmith your first 2 Master Ore and he tempers the Master Sword into Master Sword Lv2 (classically nicknamed the 'Tempered Sword'; the blade turns red), DOUBLING its attack power. You need the Master Sword (earned with the 3 Pendants) first.

GREAT_FAIRIES.name: Lorule Blacksmith (Master Sword Lv3)
GREAT_FAIRIES.location: The Blacksmith's house in Lorule — the dark-world counterpart, in the same map spot as the Hyrule smithy (reached by wall-merging through a fissure). Bring him the other 2 Master Ore (the first 2 went to the Hyrule smith; 4 exist in total).
GREAT_FAIRIES.cost: FREE — no Rupee cost. Give the Lorule Blacksmith the final 2 Master Ore and he forges Master Sword Lv3 (classically nicknamed the 'Golden Sword'; the blade turns gold), DOUBLING the attack power again — the strongest sword in the game. Requires you to have already done the Hyrule Lv2 upgrade.

GREAT_FAIRIES.name: Fairy Fountains (healing)
GREAT_FAIRIES.location: Small fairy fountains are tucked in caves across both worlds, several hidden behind obstacles that need Bombs, the Ice Rod, the Sand Rod or the Titan's Mitt to clear. Lorule has its own matching fountains, and small fairy rooms appear inside many dungeons. (Treat specific spots as a 'look for sealed caves' rule rather than a fixed list.)
GREAT_FAIRIES.cost: FREE. Touch a fairy here and it refills your hearts; or scoop one into an empty Bottle. A bottled fairy heals you when released — and if you run out of hearts while carrying one, she automatically revives you. These are your everyday 'panic heal': keep a Bottle stocked with a fairy before tough dungeons and bosses.

RUNES.name: Ravio's Bracelet
RUNES.from: Lent by Ravio after he sets up his rental shop in your house. The bracelet's full power awakens during the story (when Yuga turns Link into a painting), letting you merge at will.
RUNES.what: The game's signature move: press toward a wall to merge into it as a flat moving painting, then slide along it to cross gaps, round corners, and reach places you can't walk to. Merging drains the green Energy Gauge.
RUNES.tip: When a room looks like a dead end, try merging into the wall — almost every puzzle here is solved by becoming a painting and sliding to the other side.

RUNES.name: Captain's Sword
RUNES.from: Your first sword, picked up at the very start: the blacksmith forges it for the Captain, who leaves it behind on the table, and you set out to deliver it. This same blade is later known as the Forgotten Sword.
RUNES.what: Your starter sword. Basic slash and a charged spin attack (hold to charge, release to swing all around).
RUNES.tip: It's just a starter blade — you'll trade up to the Master Sword once you have all three Pendants of Virtue.

RUNES.name: Forgotten Sword
RUNES.from: Not a separate weapon — it's the same starter sword the Captain left behind, simply renamed 'Forgotten Sword' later in the story. The blacksmith does NOT re-forge it.
RUNES.what: The early-game sword (slash + charged spin) you carry until you claim the Master Sword.
RUNES.tip: If a guide says 'Captain's Sword' or 'Forgotten Sword,' it means this one starter weapon — there's only one early blade.

RUNES.name: Master Sword
RUNES.from: Claimed at the pedestal in the Lost Woods once you've collected all 3 Pendants of Virtue.
RUNES.what: The legendary blade — stronger than the starter sword and the key weapon for the story's foes. Fires a sword beam when your hearts are full.
RUNES.tip: Keep your hearts topped up to shoot the ranged sword beam — handy for hitting switches and enemies from safety.

RUNES.name: Master Sword Lv2
RUNES.from: Upgraded by the Hyrule blacksmith using 2 chunks of Master Ore.
RUNES.what: The first Master Sword upgrade — it doubles the blade's attack power.
RUNES.tip: Bring 2 Master Ore to the Hyrule blacksmith for this tier; you'll need 2 more (and the Lorule smith) for Lv3.

RUNES.name: Master Sword Lv3
RUNES.from: Upgraded by the Lorule blacksmith using 2 more chunks of Master Ore.
RUNES.what: The strongest sword in the game — a second doubling of attack power over Lv2.
RUNES.tip: There are exactly 4 Master Ore in the game; you need all 4 (2 for Lv2 in Hyrule, 2 for Lv3 in Lorule) to reach the top tier.

RUNES.name: Master Ore
RUNES.from: Four pieces, all in Lorule — the Thieves' Hideout, the Dark Palace, Skull Woods, and a Lorule cave reached through the fissure inside Hyrule's Sanctuary (needs the Titan's Mitt). Found, not rented.
RUNES.what: The upgrade material for the Master Sword. Two ores raise it to Lv2 (Hyrule smith), two more to Lv3 (Lorule smith); each pair doubles its power.
RUNES.tip: Collect all four before upgrading — the smiths take them two at a time, and each tier doubles your damage.

RUNES.name: Shield
RUNES.from: A basic piece of starting gear (you can buy one in town). It's plain equipment, not a rental.
RUNES.what: Hold up to block enemy attacks and projectiles. Costs no energy.
RUNES.tip: Holding the shield while standing still blocks most frontal attacks; sidestep against faster foes.

RUNES.name: Bow
RUNES.from: Rented (or later bought) from Ravio's shop.
RUNES.what: Fires arrows at distant enemies, switches, and eyes. Each shot spends green energy instead of consuming arrow ammo — there's no arrow count to manage.
RUNES.tip: Because shots cost energy, not ammo, you can fire freely — just watch the meter; it refills on its own and from Energy Potions.

RUNES.name: Bombs
RUNES.from: Rented (or later bought) from Ravio's shop.
RUNES.what: Lay an explosive that blasts cracked walls, blocks, and enemies. Each bomb spends green energy rather than a bomb count.
RUNES.tip: Look for hairline cracks in walls and floors — a bomb almost always opens a hidden room or shortcut.

RUNES.name: Fire Rod
RUNES.from: Rented (or later bought) from Ravio's shop.
RUNES.what: Shoots a burst of flame that burns enemies, lights torches, and melts ice. Each blast spends green energy.
RUNES.tip: Great against ice-themed enemies and for lighting torches fast.

RUNES.name: Ice Rod
RUNES.from: Rented (or later bought) from Ravio's shop.
RUNES.what: Fires a freezing blast that encases enemies in ice and can form ice blocks on water. Each blast spends green energy.
RUNES.tip: Frozen enemies become solid blocks — useful as stepping stones or to halt a foe while you reposition.

RUNES.name: Tornado Rod
RUNES.from: Rented (or later bought) from Ravio's shop.
RUNES.what: Whips up a whirlwind that launches Link into the air and flips/stuns nearby enemies. Each use spends green energy.
RUNES.tip: The upward launch reaches high ledges and lets you float down — handy in the House of Gales and for dodging.

RUNES.name: Sand Rod
RUNES.from: Osfala lends you one in the Thieves' Hideout, but Sheerow takes it back the moment you step outside. After you clear the Thieves' Hideout it returns to Ravio's shop, where you rent or buy your own.
RUNES.what: Raises a pillar of sand to lift Link or objects and form moving platforms across sand. Each use spends green energy.
RUNES.tip: Hold the button to extend the sand pillar as it travels — you ride it across gaps you couldn't cross on foot. You'll need it for the Desert Palace.

RUNES.name: Hammer
RUNES.from: Rented (or later bought) from Ravio's shop.
RUNES.what: A heavy smash that pounds stakes/pegs into the ground, flattens some enemies, and triggers certain switches. Each swing spends green energy.
RUNES.tip: Look for posts sticking out of the floor — the Hammer pounds them flat to open paths.

RUNES.name: Hookshot
RUNES.from: Rented (or later bought) from Ravio's shop.
RUNES.what: Fires a chain that latches onto wooden posts and anchor points to yank Link across, or pulls grabbable items to you. Each use spends green energy.
RUNES.tip: Aim at posts or blocks across a hazard to zip yourself over.

RUNES.name: Boomerang
RUNES.from: Rented (or later bought) from Ravio's shop.
RUNES.what: Throws a returning blade that stuns enemies, grabs distant items, and hits switches — then comes back. Each throw spends green energy.
RUNES.tip: Use it to collect rupees and trip faraway switches without moving.

RUNES.name: Lamp
RUNES.from: Rented (or later bought) from Ravio's shop.
RUNES.what: Lights dark rooms and ignites torches with a small flame. Each use spends green energy.
RUNES.tip: Light every torch in a dark room — it usually reveals the exit or unlocks a chest.

RUNES.name: Net
RUNES.from: Rented (or later bought) from Ravio's shop (the Bug Net).
RUNES.what: Swings to catch bugs, fairies, and bees into bottles, and can swat away some projectiles. Catching uses no ammo.
RUNES.tip: Catch a fairy in a bottle — it auto-revives you with hearts if you fall in battle.

RUNES.name: Pegasus Boots
RUNES.from: Given by the Shady Guy near Kakariko Village — merge into the wall to sneak up behind him, and he hands them over. Equipment you keep, not a rental.
RUNES.what: Hold the dash button to charge at high speed; you can plow through breakable bushes and objects and bump some enemies.
RUNES.tip: Dash into trees and posts — they often shake loose rupees, hearts, or hidden items.

RUNES.name: Power Glove
RUNES.from: Given by Rosso at the Miner's House (Hyrule, by Death Mountain) after you clear the Eastern Palace. Equipment you keep.
RUNES.what: Lets Link lift and throw small gray rocks he couldn't budge before, clearing blocked paths.
RUNES.tip: Once you have it, revisit spots blocked by liftable rocks — new shortcuts and chests open up.

RUNES.name: Titan's Mitt
RUNES.from: Upgrade of the Power Glove, found in a big chest in the Desert Palace (Lorule). Equipment you keep.
RUNES.what: Lets Link lift the heaviest rocks — the large dark stones the Power Glove can't move.
RUNES.tip: With the Titan's Mitt, go back to any oversized boulders you had to walk around earlier.

RUNES.name: Flippers
RUNES.from: Reward from Queen Oren of the Zora for returning her stolen Smooth Gem. Equipment you keep.
RUNES.what: Let Link swim in deep water instead of being pushed back at the shore, opening up lakes, rivers, and water routes.
RUNES.tip: After getting the Flippers, swim out to islands and waterways you couldn't reach before — Maiamai and other secrets hide there.

RUNES.name: Bell
RUNES.from: Given by Irene (the potion witch's granddaughter) on the bridge after you clear the Eastern Palace.
RUNES.what: Tap it (touch screen) to summon Irene, who flies you instantly to any Weather Vane you've already activated. Free fast-travel.
RUNES.tip: Activate every Weather Vane you pass — each becomes a Bell warp point, saving huge amounts of walking.

RUNES.name: Weather Vane
RUNES.from: Scattered across Hyrule and Lorule; press A at one to activate it.
RUNES.what: Save points and fast-travel destinations. Once active, you can warp to it any time using the Bell.
RUNES.tip: Touching a Weather Vane both saves your game and unlocks it as a Bell destination — never walk past one.

RUNES.name: Bottle
RUNES.from: Five total, found across Hyrule and Lorule (one bought in Kakariko, one from the Bird Lover under the bridge, one in Lorule's Vacant House, one from the Great Rupee Fairy, and one more).
RUNES.what: Empty containers you fill with potions, fairies, or bugs/bees. A bottled fairy revives you on death; potions restore hearts or energy.
RUNES.tip: Keep at least one fairy bottled before a boss — it's a free second chance if you run out of hearts.

RUNES.name: Red Potion
RUNES.from: Bought from the Witch's potion shop (stored in a Bottle).
RUNES.what: Drink to refill a large chunk of your hearts.
RUNES.tip: Stock a Red Potion before long dungeons; it's faster than hunting for heart drops mid-fight.

RUNES.name: Blue Potion
RUNES.from: Bought from the Witch's potion shop (stored in a Bottle) — the premium potion.
RUNES.what: Drink to fully restore both your hearts and your green Energy Gauge at once.
RUNES.tip: Save Blue Potion for boss fights — a full heal plus full energy in one gulp can turn a losing fight around.

RUNES.name: Energy Potion
RUNES.from: Dropped by purple pots (and some grass/enemies); stored in a Bottle.
RUNES.what: Refills the green Energy Gauge so you can keep using items and wall-merging.
RUNES.tip: When your meter runs low, smash purple pots — they're the reliable on-the-spot energy refill.

RUNES.name: Energy Gauge
RUNES.from: The shop system's shared resource, in play once you start renting items from Ravio.
RUNES.what: The green meter that powers EVERY item and Wall Merge — used in place of separate ammo (arrows, bombs, magic). It refills on its own over time and from Energy Potions.
RUNES.tip: Items don't run out of ammo here — they run out of energy. The meter refills by itself, so pause a beat to recharge instead of hunting for pickups.

RUNES.name: Stamina Scroll
RUNES.from: Found in the Ice Ruins dungeon (Lorule).
RUNES.what: Permanently enlarges your Energy Gauge, so you can use more items and merge longer before running dry.
RUNES.tip: A real, lasting upgrade — it makes every item feel more usable.

RUNES.name: Pouch
RUNES.from: Your inventory/equipment menu (touch screen) once Ravio's shop is open.
RUNES.what: Holds your items and shows the Energy Gauge. Items you only RENT can be lost if you fall in battle (Sheerow repossesses them); items you BUY are yours for good.
RUNES.tip: Buy your most-used items outright as soon as you can — bought items can't be taken away when you die, and only bought items can become 'Nice' versions.

RUNES.name: Ravio's Rental Shop
RUNES.from: Ravio sets up shop inside your house after lending you the Bracelet.
RUNES.what: Where you RENT items cheaply or, once you hold all three Pendants of Virtue, BUY them to keep. Rentals let you tackle dungeons in any order but are repossessed if you die; purchases are permanent.
RUNES.tip: Rent early to explore and find which items you love, then buy the keepers — every rental is taken at once if you fall in battle.

RUNES.name: Mother Maiamai
RUNES.from: Found in a cave near Lake Hylia; she sends you to recover her 100 Lost Maiamai across both worlds.
RUNES.what: For every 10 Lost Maiamai you return, she upgrades one item you OWN into a stronger 'Nice' version. She can't upgrade rented items.
RUNES.tip: Buy an item from Ravio before bringing it to her — she only upgrades items you own, not ones you're renting.

RUNES.name: Lost Maiamai
RUNES.from: 100 hidden across Hyrule and Lorule (often behind cracked walls, under rocks, or in merge-only spots).
RUNES.what: Collectibles you return to Mother Maiamai. Every 10 buys one 'Nice' item upgrade; returning all 100 earns the Great Spin Attack.
RUNES.tip: Many hide in wall-merge-only nooks and behind bombable cracks — sweep each area with the Bracelet and a Bomb.

RUNES.name: Nice Items
RUNES.from: Mother Maiamai upgrades, one per 10 Lost Maiamai returned (e.g. Nice Bow, Nice Bombs, Nice Fire Rod, Nice Ice Rod, Nice Tornado Rod, Nice Sand Rod, Nice Hammer, Nice Hookshot, Nice Boomerang).
RUNES.what: Buffed versions of items you own — stronger, larger, or with extra shots/spread.
RUNES.tip: Prioritize upgrading the item you lean on most; the Nice version makes tough fights and puzzles much easier.

RUNES.name: Great Spin Attack
RUNES.from: Reward from Mother Maiamai for returning all 100 Lost Maiamai.
RUNES.what: A larger, longer-reaching spin attack that hits enemies all around you from much farther out.
RUNES.tip: It's the final Maiamai prize — worth completing the hunt for the big crowd-clearing swing.

RUNES.name: Pendant of Courage
RUNES.from: A charm Princess Zelda gives Link early on; Sahasrahla later reveals it is the Pendant of Courage (after the Eastern Palace). NOT a dungeon reward.
RUNES.what: One of the three Pendants of Virtue. All three together let you claim the Master Sword.
RUNES.tip: You already hold this one before the dungeons begin — you just don't learn what it is until after the Eastern Palace.

RUNES.name: Pendant of Power
RUNES.from: Reward for clearing the Tower of Hera, atop Death Mountain (boss: Moldorm).
RUNES.what: One of the three Pendants of Virtue, needed with the other two to earn the Master Sword.
RUNES.tip: You can tackle the early dungeons in any order thanks to Ravio's rentals — just collect all three pendants.

RUNES.name: Pendant of Wisdom
RUNES.from: Reward for clearing the House of Gales, in the southeast lake region (boss: Margomill).
RUNES.what: The third Pendant of Virtue. With Courage and Power, it opens the path to the Master Sword in the Lost Woods.
RUNES.tip: The House of Gales leans on the Tornado Rod — rent it before you go in. Once you hold all three pendants, head to the Lost Woods pedestal to pull the Master Sword.

CHAMPIONS.name: Pendant of Courage
CHAMPIONS.note: Won by clearing the Eastern Palace — one of the three Pendants of Virtue needed for the Master Sword.

CHAMPIONS.name: Pendant of Wisdom
CHAMPIONS.note: Won by clearing the House of Gales out on the lake — the second Pendant of Virtue.

CHAMPIONS.name: Pendant of Power
CHAMPIONS.note: Won atop the Tower of Hera on Death Mountain — the third Pendant. With all three, draw the Master Sword.

CHAMPIONS.name: Sage Gulley
CHAMPIONS.note: Free the painted Sage Gulley from the Dark Palace — one of the seven Sages you must rescue across Lorule.

CHAMPIONS.name: Sage Oren
CHAMPIONS.note: Free Sage Oren, the Zora queen, from the Swamp Palace.

CHAMPIONS.name: Sage Seres
CHAMPIONS.note: Free Sage Seres from the Skull Woods in Lorule's dark forest.

CHAMPIONS.name: Sage Osfala
CHAMPIONS.note: Free Sage Osfala from the Thieves' Hideout (with Thief Girl's help).

CHAMPIONS.name: Sage Rosso
CHAMPIONS.note: Free Sage Rosso from the frozen Ice Ruins.

CHAMPIONS.name: Sage Irene
CHAMPIONS.note: Free Sage Irene from the Desert Palace in the Lorule sands.

CHAMPIONS.name: Sage Impa
CHAMPIONS.note: Free Sage Impa from Turtle Rock — the seventh Sage opens the way to Lorule Castle.

ROADMAP.name: The Three Pendants
ROADMAP.note: Courage, Wisdom, and Power — won from the Eastern Palace, the House of Gales, and the Tower of Hera. Carry all three to the Lost Woods pedestal to draw the Master Sword.

ROADMAP.name: The Seven Sages
ROADMAP.note: Yuga has sealed seven Sages into paintings across Lorule's dungeons. Free all seven to open Lorule Castle and confront Yuga Ganon.

ROADMAP.name: Mother Maiamai's 100 Babies
ROADMAP.note: One hundred baby Maiamai are hidden across Hyrule and Lorule (often behind walls you must merge into). Return them to Mother Maiamai to upgrade your items into stronger 'Nice' versions.

ROADMAP.name: Pieces of Heart
ROADMAP.note: Twenty-eight are hidden across both worlds. Every four extend your life by a full heart.

TIPS.name: Merge into the walls
items: Ravio's Bracelet lets you press flat into a wall and slide along it as a moving painting — through barred grates, around corners, and onto ledges you can't walk to. It's the answer to most 'how do I get there?' puzzles.
Merging spends your green energy meter; it refills almost instantly when you step back out, so merge often.

You also cross between Hyrule and Lorule by MERGING into the purple wall fissures (and later via the warp paintings). Lorule is the same map, twisted — a wall you can't pass in one world may be open in the other.

TIPS.name: Ravio's rental shop
items: Unlike older Zeldas, you don't find your tools in dungeons — Ravio's Shop (in your house) RENTS you almost every item (Bow, Hammer, Bombs, Hookshot, Tornado Rod, Fire/Ice Rod, Sand Rod…) cheaply, up front, in almost any order.
The catch: if you DIE, Ravio's bird repossesses every rented item until you re-rent them. Once you can afford it, BUY your favorites so a death can't disarm you.
Because items aren't gated, you can tackle the dungeons in a fairly free order — rent what a dungeon needs and go.

TIPS.name: Energy, not ammo
items: Items don't use consumable ammo here — the Bow, rods, Hammer, Bombs and merging all drain one shared GREEN energy meter that recharges on its own.
So fire and merge freely; just pace yourself in a fight so you don't bottom the meter out mid-combo.
Pieces of Heart raise life; the meter grows a little as you progress and via some upgrades.

COLLECTIBLES.label: Pieces of Heart
COLLECTIBLES.note: Every 4 make a full Heart Container.

COLLECTIBLES.label: Maiamai
COLLECTIBLES.note: Hidden across Hyrule & Lorule (many behind merge-walls); return them to Mother Maiamai for 'Nice' item upgrades.
